proof_sketch: blockDim.x=256 → (256+31)/32 = 8 (integer). Original: lane_id < 8 → threads 0-7 read valid warp_maxs[0-7], threads 8-31 get -FLT_MAX. Mutated: lane_id <= 8 → thread 8 reads warp_maxs[8], which is never written (only warps 0-7 write indices 0-7). warp_maxs[8] contains uninitialized garbage. The difference in output occurs only if that garbage exceeds the true max of the valid entries. Since garbage is not determined by input values, no input pattern can guarantee a different result.

The difference in output occurs only if that garbage exceeds the true max of the valid entries. Since garbage is not determined by input values, no input pattern can guarantee a different result.",
  "survival_reason": "The mutation changes the condition from `lane_id < 8` to `lane_id <= 8` in the final reduction of the dim2 kernel. This causes lane_id=8 (thread index 8 in warp 0) to read from shared memory index 8 of `warp_maxs`, which is never written because only 8 warps (indices 0-7) exist. The read returns undefined garbage. Whether this garbage affects the final max depends on its value relative to the true maximum. Since the garbage value cannot be controlled by varying input tensor values, any fixed input may or may not trigger a difference across runs. The test harness ran many value-distribution policies (sparse, extreme, denormal, etc.) but all passed because the garbage happened to not exceed the true max in those executions. No value-level test can deterministically force the garbage to be larger, so the mutant is unkillable under the fixed-shape contract.",

  "kill_strategy": "No value-level strategy can reliably trigger a difference because the behavior depends on uninitialized shared memory, not on input values.",
  "suggested_test": null,
  "recommendations": "Ensure that shared memory indices are always within the bounds of written values. In particular, the condition `lane_id < (blockDim.x+31)/32` correctly limits reads to indices 0-7; the mutation `<=` introduces an out-of-bounds read. Kernel robustness can be improved by explicitly initializing all shared memory entries before use, or by adding a safety check that clamps the index."
